question: You finish a difficult day feeling flat. You cannot identify why. The most useful first move is...
option_a: Push through it — everyone has bad days
option_b: Analyse every interaction until you find the cause
option_c: Notice it without trying to fix or explain it. The cause usually surfaces once you stop chasing it
explanation: Trying to immediately diagnose a feeling often pushes it underground. Noticing without explaining is a skill. The cause becomes clear more often when you stop forcing it.
hint: You do not have to understand a feeling to acknowledge it.

question: You are in a meeting and your body tenses before your brain has worked out why. That physical signal is...
option_b: Real data — your system detected something before your conscious mind caught up
explanation: The body's threat detection is faster than conscious thought. That pre-cognitive tension is information. It is not always right. But it is always worth noting.
hint: Your body processed something before your brain did. What was it?

question: After tracking your energy for a week you notice some tasks drain you regardless of preparation, others energise you regardless of how tired you started. This tells you...
option_b: How your brain actually works — and it is one of the most useful things you can know about yourself
explanation: Energy response patterns are consistent and personal. They are not random. Knowing your map means you can make decisions that fit your actual architecture rather than fighting it.
hint: Where does your energy go? Where does it come from?

question: You notice a strong reaction every time someone questions your competence — even mildly, even when they are probably right. This most likely means...
option_b: Your competence became load-bearing for your identity at some point — probably when other things felt uncertain
explanation: When a quality becomes the thing we rely on to feel okay, any challenge to it triggers a response that is bigger than the situation warrants. Knowing which qualities are load-bearing for you is important self-knowledge.
hint: What would it mean about you if that were not true?

question: You are new somewhere. In your first month the most valuable thing to map is...
option_a: The org chart and formal reporting lines
option_b: The informal network — who people actually go to, regardless of title
explanation: Every organisation has two structures. The formal one is on the org chart. The real one is built from relationships, history and informal influence — and it rarely matches the chart.
hint: Who do people actually go to when something needs to happen?

question: You have been invisible in your team for several months. Not disliked — just absent. The smallest action that begins to change this is...
option_a: A presentation showing your full capabilities
option_b: Staying later so results speak for themselves
option_c: One genuine contribution — a question, an observation, a specific piece of help — to one person, once
option_d: Explaining to your manager why you have been quiet
explanation: Visibility is rebuilt through small consistent moments, not grand gestures. One real contribution shifts the dynamic slightly. Then another. The accumulation is what makes you present.
hint: One real thing. One person. Once. That is enough to start.

question: Someone senior makes a dismissive comment about your work in front of others. The response that serves you best is...
option_a: Defend yourself clearly so everyone understands your position
option_b: Go quiet — picking a fight with someone senior is not worth it
option_c: A calm, brief response that neither concedes nor escalates — then move on
option_d: Ask them to clarify what they meant
explanation: Both instinctive responses — defend or collapse — hand control to the other person. A calm brief response that does not concede and does not escalate demonstrates that the comment did not move you. That is the most useful signal you can send.
hint: Neither fight nor flight. Something steadier than both.

question: Your organisation has an unwritten rule you dislike — information shared selectively, knowledge used as currency. Your most effective response is...
option_c: Understand the rule clearly enough to navigate it without being naive — while deciding privately how much you are willing to operate it
explanation: Understanding a rule you dislike is not endorsing it. You cannot navigate a system you refuse to look at. Knowing how it works lets you protect yourself from being disadvantaged by it.
hint: You can see a system clearly without becoming it.

question: Someone you respect says something critical that is partly true and partly unfair. The clearest response is...
option_a: Reject it all because part was unfair
option_b: Accept it all because some was true
option_c: Separate the two — take what is accurate, set aside what is not, without letting the unfair part contaminate the valid part
explanation: Mixed feedback is the hardest kind. It requires holding two responses at once — openness to the valid signal and discernment to reject the noise. Collapsing either way costs you something.
hint: Take the signal. Leave the noise.

question: You have something important to say in a meeting. You notice you speak faster and louder when uncertain. The adjustment that actually helps is...
option_a: Speak louder so you cannot be talked over
option_b: Prepare more so you feel less uncertain
option_c: Slow down — pace signals certainty, and slowing down when uncertain is counterintuitive but effective
explanation: Volume and speed under pressure signal anxiety to a room. Slowing your pace when uncertain does the opposite of what you expect — it reads as control to everyone listening.
hint: Slow down. The room will wait.

question: You are asked to do something not illegal but uncomfortable. The most honest question before deciding is...
option_b: Is it worth the career risk of refusing
option_c: Is this something I am willing to do — and if I do it, what does that make me here
explanation: The question of whether you will do something uncomfortable is ultimately a question about identity. Will anyone find out and is it worth the risk are consequence questions. They do not tell you who you are.
hint: Not what will happen. Who will you be if you do it?

question: Your organisation rewards visible behaviour — confident presentations, being seen to be busy — that has little to do with actual quality. You have options...
option_a: Refuse to play — quality speaks for itself
option_b: Play it fully — give them what they reward
option_c: Do enough of the visible to be taken seriously, while putting your real energy into the work that matters
option_d: Leave any organisation that operates this way
explanation: Most institutions reward a combination of real contribution and performed contribution. Refusing all performance means your real work goes unseen. The sophisticated move is enough visibility to earn the room to do the real work.
hint: Enough performance to be trusted. Enough reality to be worth trusting.

question: Someone attempts to belittle you in a room. You feel the instinct to shrink. What you do instead is...
option_b: Stay physically still, say something brief and factual, redirect the conversation
explanation: The comment is a test of whether you can be destabilised. The room watches your response more than the comment. Matching their energy gives them what they wanted. Stillness and a brief factual response signals that the comment did not move you.
hint: They need your reaction. Do not give it to them.

question: Walking into a room where someone will challenge you. The internal state that serves you best is...
option_a: Aggression — ready to push back hard
option_c: Detachment — not caring about the outcome
option_d: The settled certainty of someone who knows who they are and does not need this room to confirm it
explanation: Aggression needs constant fuel. Preparation is useful but not enough. Detachment can become avoidance. The state that actually protects you is the one that does not need external validation — not from the room, not from the outcome.
hint: You do not need this room to tell you who you are.

question: After a period of withdrawal the hardest part of re-engaging is not finding the right words. It is...
option_c: Tolerating being seen again before your nervous system has caught up with your decision
explanation: Re-entry is not primarily a social skills problem. It is a nervous system problem. Your thinking brain decides to re-engage. Your nervous system — which learned that engagement leads to loss — is not immediately convinced. Tolerating that gap, briefly and repeatedly, is how the system updates.
hint: Your decision to re-engage is ahead of your nervous system. That gap is temporary.
